headline: James Wood Is Built Different, and Other Lessons From Beer League Week 3
subheadline: The Athletic Park Dragons unleashed an absolute monster performance, the Hiroshima Radiation went full Thanos, and somehow we got a tie in 2025.
body:
## The Week Nobody Saw Coming

Let me set the scene. It's Week 3. We're still in that early-season window where everyone thinks they're a contender, everyone's checking their lineup three times a day like they're defusing a bomb, and then James Wood goes out and puts up a line so absurd it looks like someone left a cheat code running. Eight runs. Three home runs. Eight RBI. Two steals. A .655 OBP. A *point-six-five-five* OBP. That's not a fantasy baseball stat — that's a video game difficulty setting someone forgot to turn off. (For context, a .655 OBP in a single week means James Wood was basically refusing to make outs on a philosophical level.)

The **Athletic Park Dragons** didn't just win their matchup against the **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** — they sent a message. When you pair Wood's historic week with Willson Contreras going absolutely feral at 1B (.607 OBP, 2 HR, 9 RBI — nine! — for Boston), and Dansby Swanson quietly going 8 runs, 1 HR, 2 RBI, the Dragons looked like the '27 Yankees playing a rec league team. The Reapers weren't embarrassed — Taylor Ward hit .464 OBP for Baltimore, Willi Castro was useful, Maikel Garcia contributed — but some weeks you just run into a buzzsaw. This was a buzzsaw week. Final: 6-3 Dragons.

## Four Matchups Worth Breaking Down

The **Sugar Land Skeeters** are already making the rest of the league nervous, and they should be. An 8-2 demolition of the **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** is the kind of result that makes you rethink your offseason trades at 11pm on a Tuesday. Matt Olson went full Matt Olson (7 runs, 2 HR, 5 RBI, .393 OBP), Drake Baldwin looked like a catcher who wandered into the wrong era (5 runs, 1 HR, 5 RBI), and Max Muncy — who people have been sleeping on for two years now — went 6 runs, 3 HR, 3 RBI. The Twins weren't cooked, though. Brandon Nimmo (2 HR, 5 RBI, .385 OBP) and Alec Burleson (.423 OBP, 5 RBI) gave it a real go. Jonathan Aranda's .231 OBP, though? That's the kind of number that ends friendships in fantasy leagues.

Meanwhile, the most morally complicated matchup of the week was **BaseballPro77** edging **North Correa** 5-3, which feels wrong given that North Correa had SHOHEI OHTANI (3 HR, 4 RBI, .467 OBP), Oneil Cruz running wild with SIX steals, and Nico Hoerner quietly posting 7 RBI. That should be enough to win. But Jordan Walker went full Jordan Walker Awakening Tour (5 HR, 6 runs, 6 RBI, .407 OBP), Austin Riley was Austin Riley (.458 OBP, 6 runs, 4 RBI), and Bobby Witt Jr. swiped four bags. Sometimes the fantasy gods are cruel and indifferent, like a season finale that kills your favorite character for no reason.

The **Riverside Roughnecks** beating the **Benchwarmers** 6-3 deserves a mention mostly because Trevor Story — Trevor Story! — put up 9 RBI. Nine. Story has been the fantasy equivalent of a guy who shows up to a party three hours late but somehow wins beer pong. The Benchwarmers had real contributors (Kevin McGonigle, 6 runs; Ceddanne Rafaela, .500 OBP), but 9 RBI from your shortstop is hard to overcome. Jackson Merrill also had three steals for the Roughnecks, which felt very 'quietly useful guy in a heist movie.'

## The Performers of the Week

Three guys need their own paragraph. First: **James Wood**. Already covered, but 3 HR / 8 RBI / .655 OBP is the kind of week you screenshot and send to the group chat with zero context. Second: **Elly De La Cruz**, who posted 7 runs, 2 HR, 5 RBI, and 4 steals for the **Buttville Skidmarks** in a week that ended in a 4-4 tie against **General Hospital** — which, honestly, feels like exactly the kind of ending Elly De La Cruz would be frustrated about. (Four steals and you STILL tie? Brutal.) Third: **Cam Schlittler** of General Hospital threw 10 innings, struck out 15, and almost carried his team to a win. A 5.40 ERA kept him from being a hero, but 15 K's in 10 innings is appointment fantasy television.

## Standings & Race

With the standings still sorting themselves out in these early weeks, the **Sugar Land Skeeters** and **Athletic Park Dragons** are clearly announcing themselves as the teams nobody wants to face right now. The Skeeters have the lineup depth of a team that simply refuses to have a bad week. The Dragons have James Wood, and James Wood appears to be operating on a different plane of existence. The **Hiroshima Radiation** deserve credit too — an 8-2 win over the **Canberra Wombats** despite Gunnar Henderson going nuclear (4 HR, 6 RBI, 2 SB) for the losing side is genuinely impressive. Bryce Harper's .640 OBP tells you everything you need to know about why the Radiation won that one. Meanwhile, the Wombats are learning that a league-best player on your roster doesn't guarantee anything. That's a lesson the **North Correa** manager also learned this week. Shohei Ohtani with 3 HR and a .467 OBP. Still lost. Fantasy is merciless and I love it.

## Looking Ahead

Next week, all eyes are on what the **Buttville Skidmarks** and **General Hospital** do after their humiliating 4-4 draw — both teams will be desperate, which makes for great fantasy television. (Think Game 7 energy, but with more spreadsheets.) The **Canberra Wombats** need to figure out how they lose with Gunnar Henderson going off, because that's a roster that should be winning. If they drop another matchup next week, the 'talented-but-snakebitten' narrative starts writing itself. See you on the other side.

## Waiver Wire

The biggest splash of the week belongs to **Benchwarmers**, who dropped Zach Eflin and Josh Smith to scoop up Spencer Arrighetti, Graham Ashcraft, Mauricio Dubón, and Garrett Mitchell — a four-player haul that screams "I've completely blown up my roster and I'm not sorry about it," which is either a genius pivot or the fantasy equivalent of flipping the Monopoly board. **North Correa** made the most eyebrow-raising subtraction, cutting Jack Flaherty loose only to watch **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** swoop him up immediately alongside Angel Martínez and Justin Sterner, while also parting ways with Masyn Winn — a move that suggests the Wonderdogs are going all-in on upside over the stretch run. **Albuquerque Bombers** went full bullpen makeover, dropping Aaron Ashby, Steven Okert, and Grant Taylor to bring in Adrian Morejon, Bryan Abreu, and Orion Kerkering, which is either a savvy relief corps upgrade or a very bold bet that three new arms are better than three old problems. Meanwhile, **Buttville Skidmarks** adding Aaron Civale and Keaton Winn while dropping Brandon Sproat is the classic "I need rotation stability right now and I'll worry about the future later" move that defines the second half of every fantasy season.
